at fault: Determine the conclusion of the following passage: Nietzsche argues that psychology has been trapped by moral prejudices and fears and that few have understood it as the unfolding of the will to power; moreover, once one treats hatred, envy, covetousness, and the lust to rule as normal conditions and necessary components of life, one becomes disoriented and loses oneself, effectively moving beyond conventional morality, so that psychology again opens the way to fundamental problems?

Which statement best expresses the argument’s conclusion that psychology ultimately returns to addressing foundational issues when one abandons moral prejudices and recognizes hatred, envy, covetousness, and the lust to rule as life-conditions and essential forces?

Identify the conclusion of the following argument. All of psychology has gotten stuck in moral prejudices and fears. No one has come close to understanding it as the development of the will to power. However, if a person even begins to regard the effects of hatred, envy, covetousness, and the lust to rule as conditions of life and furthermore, as factors essential to the general economy of life, he will begin to get seasick. At this point, he begins to lose himself, and sail over morality. Thus, psychology becomes again the path to fundamental problems. (Friedrich Nietzsche, Beyond Good and Evil)

Articulate which statement functions as the argument’s conclusion: All of psychology has become mired in moral prejudices and fears. No one has come close to conceiving it as the development of the will to power. Yet when someone begins to view hatred, envy, covetousness, and the lust to rule as ordinary conditions of life and as indispensable elements in the overall economy of life, he will start to feel seasick; at that juncture he loses himself and sails beyond morality, so that psychology once more serves as the route to fundamental problems?
